11 min read
Just now

--

The exact solo-HR AI stack, the order to buy it, what each tool really costs, the week-one setup, and the hiring-bias laws that can quietly sink you. No fluff, no affiliate links.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The best people person I know almost walked out last spring. Not because she was bad at the job. Because she was one human being asked to do the work of a department, and the math never closed. She was the recruiter, the onboarder, the payroll fixer, the culture committee, and the compliance officer, all at a company that had grown past the point where one person could actually hold all of it. She lost a great engineer because a competitor sent an offer while she was still scheduling the second-round panel. She found out someone was quitting the week they gave notice, not a month before when it could have been fixed. Nothing about her judgment was the problem. Her coverage was.
I run an independent reviews site. I do not resell a single tool in this piece, I take no commission when you buy one, and when a post on my site is sponsored it says so at the top and the sponsorship never moves a score. So I can tell you the thing the vendor demos bury: for a team of one, the gap between you and a real HR department was never talent. It was that a department can source, screen, onboard, run payroll, keep people engaged, and stay inside employment law all at the same time, and one person cannot. In 2026 that gap closed. Four tools now cover the four jobs a department used to, for less than a company spends on office coffee. Here is exactly what to buy, in what order, what it really costs, and the parts that can get you sued if you are careless.
The long version, with every tool ranked by category, is on my site: the best AI tools for HR in 2026.
The real reason you are drowning
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The coverage gap, in numbers: what one HR person is actually up against.Start with the number that runs hiring: the best candidates are gone in about ten days. Strong people are not sitting in your pipeline waiting. They are interviewing at three places at once, and the first company to move a good process quickly is usually the one that closes them. Everything downstream, the panel, the offer, the start date, only matters if you are still in the conversation when they decide.
Now overlay a solo HR person’s actual week. A great resume lands on Tuesday. You are mid onboarding for two new hires, a manager needs a termination handled correctly, and payroll runs Thursday. You get to that resume on Monday. You have already lost most of your odds, and you did nothing wrong except be one person doing one thing at a time. A department never has that gap because someone is always moving the next candidate forward. That single structural disadvantage, repeated across every req and every new hire, is most of the difference between a company that recruits well and one that limps. It is also the disadvantage AI erases most completely, which is why the stack starts with hiring and not with anything softer.
Job one: hire like you have a recruiting team
Half the job of a department is just moving candidates through a process without dropping any of them, and that is exactly what an applicant tracking system with AI does. It parses every resume, ranks candidates against the role, writes the job post, schedules interviews, keeps every applicant warm with timely updates, and gives you one place where nothing falls through a crack. For a team of one, the ATS is not a nice-to-have. It is the thing that lets you run five open roles without losing a candidate to silence.
Here is the part the demos skip: pick the tool for how structured you want to be, not for the longest feature list. Some systems are built around disciplined, bias-reducing hiring with scorecards and structured interviews, which is the right default when a bad hire is expensive and you cannot afford a messy process. Others are lighter and faster to set up when you are hiring at volume and want speed over ceremony. I walk through the whole field, and who each one actually fits, in my roundup of the best applicant tracking systems, and I put the two most common finalists head to head in Greenhouse vs Lever, with the full write-ups in my Greenhouse review and Lever review.
The other half of hiring is writing, and it eats your week. Job descriptions, outreach, rejection notes, offer letters. A general AI model already handles most of it for around twenty dollars a month, and purpose-built job-description tools go further by flagging biased or exclusionary language before it ever goes live. That last part matters more than it sounds, because the wording of a job post is one of the easiest places to accidentally narrow your pool or invite a complaint. I cover the dedicated options in my roundup of the best AI job description generators, and I lay out the exact prompt-and-review workflow in how to write job descriptions with AI. Set your ATS up in week one, connect your calendar, and write five reusable templates once. After that, hiring stops being the thing that owns your calendar.
Job two: onboard and run the admin without a back office
The moment someone says yes, a second department’s worth of work begins. Offer letter, background check, payroll setup, benefits enrollment, device and account provisioning, tax forms, and the first-week schedule. Miss one piece and a new hire’s first impression is chaos, or worse, their first paycheck is wrong. A department has a people-ops coordinator for this. Alone, you have a human resource information system, and it is the single highest-leverage tool after the ATS.
The right HRIS turns onboarding into a checklist that runs itself: it collects the paperwork, kicks off payroll, provisions the accounts, and tracks time off, so you stop being the bottleneck for every routine request. The real decision is domestic versus global. If your whole team is in one country, a broad HRIS that bundles payroll, benefits, and device management is the cleaner fit. If you are hiring contractors or employees across borders, you want a platform built for international payroll and compliance so you are not stitching together five vendors and hoping the tax handling is right. I break down that exact split in my roundup of the best HRIS software, with the two platforms most teams weigh covered in my Rippling review and Deel review, and the direct decision in Rippling vs Deel. Budget realistically here, because HRIS pricing is usually per employee per month and climbs with headcount and add-on modules, so price it at the size you will be in a year, not the size you are today.
Job three: keep the people you worked so hard to hire
Hiring is expensive and quitting is worse. Replacing someone can cost between half and twice their annual salary once you count the search, the ramp, and the lost momentum, and the person who leaves is almost never a surprise to a good HR team. It is a surprise to an overloaded one, because nobody was watching the signals. A department runs engagement surveys and performance cycles that catch the quiet flight risk months early. Alone, you skip both, not from neglect but because there is never a free afternoon to build them.
Two tools cover this job, and they pay for themselves the first time they save one resignation. An engagement platform runs lightweight, regular pulse surveys, reads the sentiment, and tells you where morale is slipping before it becomes a resignation letter, which is the whole point for a solo operator: an early-warning system you do not have to staff. A performance management tool runs the review and goal cycles on a schedule, nudges managers, and keeps a real record of who is growing and who is stuck, so raises and promotions rest on evidence instead of on who spoke up loudest. I compare the field in my roundups of the best employee engagement software and the best AI performance management software. Start with engagement if retention is your bleeding wound, and add performance once the review season is what is eating you.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The four jobs a department does, and the one tool that covers each.If you run a staffing agency, not an in-house desk
One note before the compliance part, because a chunk of the people reading this are not the first HR hire at a startup. You run a small staffing or recruiting agency, and your problem is the opposite: not five roles, but hundreds of candidates and dozens of client requisitions at once. The stack shifts. You want an agency-grade platform that combines an applicant tracking system with a candidate relationship manager and the recruiting-specific automation to keep a huge talent pool warm and matched to open orders. That is a different tool from an in-house ATS, and I cover the category, and who leads it, in my roundup of the best AI tools for staffing agencies, with the market standard broken down in my Bullhorn review. Everything below about compliance applies to you just as hard, arguably harder, because you are making selection decisions on behalf of other employers.
The hiring laws that can actually sink you
This is the section the demos will never show you, and it is the one that separates a tool that helps from a tool that gets you sued. The moment you let AI screen, rank, or score human beings, you step into employment law, and the rules are multiplying fast.
Start with the oldest and most important principle, because it predates AI and applies no matter what tool you use. Under federal anti-discrimination law, if a selection process screens out a protected group at a meaningfully higher rate, that is disparate impact, and it is illegal even when nobody intended it and even when a machine did the screening. The rough rule of thumb regulators use is the four-fifths rule: if your tool passes one group at less than eighty percent of the rate it passes the highest group, you have a problem to investigate. An AI screener trained on your past hires can quietly reproduce every bias in that history at scale, and “the algorithm did it” is not a defense. The federal guidance is explicit that you, the employer, are on the hook for the tools you use.
Then there is the newer wave of state and city law aimed directly at hiring AI. New York City now requires an independent annual bias audit of automated employment decision tools used on candidates, published results, and advance notice to applicants. Illinois requires notice and consent before you use AI to analyze video interviews, and has moved to restrict AI in employment decisions that produce a discriminatory effect. More states are passing versions of this every year, and the European Union’s AI Act classifies recruitment and selection AI as high-risk, with real documentation duties, which matters the moment you hire anyone in Europe. You do not need to memorize every statute. You need to assume that if a tool makes or heavily influences a hiring decision, some regulator expects you to be able to prove it is fair.
So here is the standing rule that keeps you safe, and it is not complicated. Keep a human, you, on every consequential decision, and never let a tool auto-reject a candidate with no person in the loop. Before you buy any screening or scoring tool, ask the vendor one direct question: can you give me your bias-audit documentation and tell me exactly what data this model was trained on. If they cannot, walk away, because you are the one who inherits the liability, not them. Disclose to candidates when AI is part of your process. And make reasonable accommodation easy, because AI video and assessment tools can disadvantage people with disabilities, and the law expects you to offer an alternative. None of this is a reason to avoid the tools. It is the reason to keep a person accountable for anything a candidate or employee experiences.
